title: What working in Strategy actually involves
summary:
Strategy is not inspirational planning detached from reality. It is the work of deciding where to focus, what to ignore, and how to sequence decisions under uncertainty. In practice, strategists gather signals, frame the real problem, compare options, and turn ambiguity into a path other people can actually follow.

The role is demanding because almost every strategic decision is made before certainty exists. You rarely get perfect information, and the cost of a bad call can compound quietly. Strong strategists therefore build better inputs, sharper tradeoff logic, and clearer narratives that help teams move without false confidence.

What makes the role powerful is that it shapes direction rather than just output. Good strategy changes what the business notices, which opportunities it pursues, and which mistakes it avoids.
coreWork: Researching markets, competitors, customers, and internal realities before making big calls.
Framing strategic choices around tradeoffs, sequencing, and resource constraints.
Building scenarios, decision narratives, and priority architecture the broader team can use.
Revisiting assumptions before drift turns yesterday's plan into today's blind spot.
frontierPull: People are drawn to strategy because it sits close to leverage. You get to ask the biggest questions in the room: where to compete, what matters most, and which bets actually deserve time, money, and attention.
whenItCameAbout: The strategy role emerged as organizations grew too complex to run on intuition alone. Modern strategy combines classic planning, market analysis, operating judgment, and communication so leadership can make coherent decisions under changing conditions.
whatPeopleDo: Scan markets, competitors, and customer movement for signals worth acting on.
Turn messy information into clear choices and sequencing.
Pressure-test assumptions with scenarios and downside thinking.
Explain the strategic logic so leadership and operators can move together.
howPeopleGetThere: People usually arrive through consulting, product, finance, operations, founder work, or research-heavy leadership roles. The strongest path is not credentials alone but repeated exposure to real choices where tradeoffs had consequences.
whoThrivesHere: People do well when they can think clearly without becoming abstract, stay grounded in evidence, and communicate tradeoffs in a way that others can actually use.
exposures: The risk of elegant theory that fails operationally
careerLevers: Improving research quality and signal intake
Getting better at sequencing, not just ideation
Developing stronger commercial and institutional judgment
